rationale: The citing paper repeatedly uses Hoeffler (2003) as a core conceptual base for uncertainty in really new product adoption, explicitly adopting its uncertainty dimensions (performance, symbolic, switching-cost, affective) for theory development and hypothesis framing. It also uses Hoeffler's approach in study design decisions, including newness assessment and coding/measurement of uncertainty constructs. In the General Discussion, the authors position their contribution as building on Hoeffler by showing how these uncertainties shift with temporal distance and can be managed via mental simulation, indicating a clear functional dependence beyond a passing mention.
relation_description: The citing paper extends Hoeffler (2003) by taking its uncertainty framework for really new products and adding temporal distance and mental-simulation communication strategies as key contingencies. It preserves Hoeffler's core uncertainty constructs while developing new predictions and evidence about when different uncertainties dominate and how to reduce them.
